key_insight: W–B nanoclusters show highly non-monotonic stability with a few “magic” compositions, and their tungsten sites can bind and activate ammonia for the first N–H cleavage.
engineering_relevance: These results identify W–B nanoclusters as tunable non-precious catalysts whose composition and local structure can be engineered to control ammonia decomposition activity.
method: Evolutionary search was combined with DFT to map ground-state structures of W$_m$B$_n$ clusters up to 43 atoms.
Adsorption calculations evaluated NH$_3$ and H binding on candidate clusters.
Nudged elastic band simulations estimated the activation barrier for the first N–H bond cleavage.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports isolated stability maxima at WB$_{16}$, W$_2$B$_8$, W$_7$B$_{24}$, and W$_{11}$B$_{22}$, NH$_3$ adsorption energies of -0.54 to -1.78 eV, and N–H cleavage barriers of 1.1–1.4 eV.
watch_item: Catalytic performance is inferred from cluster-scale DFT and NEB results, so real-world behavior may depend on temperature, support effects, and cluster sintering.
paper_title: Insights of Ammonia Decomposition on W--B Nanoclusters by Computational Simulations
